paragraph: Northern Cliffland University broke ground last Tuesday on its long-anticipated Veymarr Sciences Pavilion, a 142,000-square-foot facility that will house the university's consolidated chemistry, biochemistry, and materials science programs under one roof for the first time in institutional history. Provost Delaine Forthwick announced that the $87.4 million project is funded through a combination of a $42 million lead gift from the Orveston Family Charitable Trust, $31 million in state capital appropriations, and $14.4 million drawn from the university's infrastructure reserve. The pavilion is expected to accommodate 1,200 undergraduate laboratory enrollments per semester, double the current capacity, and will include six interdisciplinary wet-lab suites reserved for faculty-student collaborative research. Architects from the Kelloman-Pryce Group designed the structure to meet Tier-2 sustainability certification, incorporating passive cooling systems and rooftop photovoltaic arrays expected to offset 38 percent of the building's annual energy consumption. Completion is projected for the spring of 2028, with full occupancy beginning the following academic year. Dean of Natural Sciences Percival Houghton called the pavilion the most consequential investment in the sciences division in four decades and noted it would allow the university to expand graduate enrollment in chemistry by approximately 60 students annually.

Halberg State College announced the appointment of Dr. Simone Carravaux as the inaugural holder of the Wyndmere Endowed Chair in Public Policy, a position established through a $5.2 million gift from alumnus Garrett Wyndmere, a retired logistics executive who graduated from Halberg in 1979. Dr. Carravaux, who previously directed the Center for Civic Futures at Eastmoor Metropolitan University, specializes in subnational fiscal policy and intergovernmental relations, with a particular focus on revenue-sharing frameworks between state agencies and municipal governments. Her appointment, effective the coming fall semester, is expected to bolster Halberg's recently launched master's program in public administration, which enrolled its inaugural cohort of 34 students last autumn. The Wyndmere Chair carries an annual research stipend of $85,000 and funds one postdoctoral research associate and two graduate research fellowships per year. Provost Anneliese Drombreck praised the hire as transformative for a program that has seen a 47 percent increase in applications over the past three years. Dr. Carravaux has published two monographs and 28 peer-reviewed articles in journals focusing on governmental finance and is expected to launch a multiyear grant-funded project examining property tax equity across rural districts within her first year at Halberg.

The Korvex Honors Program at Westlake Collegiate Institute awarded its annual Scholars Distinction prizes last week, recognizing 12 undergraduate students for exceptional independent research completed during the 2025-2026 academic year. The top prize, the Korvex Gold Medal, was awarded to senior Francesca Almeda for her thesis examining sediment transport dynamics in artificially managed river deltas, research she conducted over three semesters with funding from the university's Undergraduate Discovery Grant program. Almeda's 112-page thesis was evaluated by a five-member interdisciplinary panel and described by reviewers as exhibiting methodological rigor equivalent to graduate-level scholarship. The remaining 11 students received Silver and Bronze distinctions across disciplines ranging from comparative literature to biostatistics. In total, the 2025-2026 cohort produced 12 theses averaging 89 pages each, and three students have already submitted manuscript versions of their work to peer-reviewed journals for review. Westlake's honors program currently enrolls 340 students across all four class years, representing approximately 8 percent of the undergraduate population. Program Director Nathaniel Forsythe noted that this year's class achieved the highest average evaluation score in the program's 22-year history, with a composite panel rating of 4.61 out of 5.

The Ravenspar County Unified School District announced a sweeping revision to its K-12 mathematics curriculum framework, the most comprehensive restructuring since the district adopted a standards-based model 14 years ago. Under the new framework, approved 7-2 by the school board at its monthly session, all elementary schools will introduce algebraic reasoning concepts beginning in third grade rather than fifth, and middle school students will have access to a redesigned accelerated pathway leading to advanced coursework by eighth grade. The district's curriculum director, Margolaine Trescott, said the changes are grounded in a three-year internal analysis of student performance data showing that students who encountered algebraic thinking before sixth grade scored an average of 18 percentile points higher on statewide mathematics assessments by ninth grade. Implementation will begin in the 2026-2027 school year following a summer of professional development for approximately 340 classroom teachers. The district has budgeted $2.1 million for training, new instructional materials, and ongoing coaching support. Ravenspar serves 21,400 students across 31 schools and has a four-year high school graduation rate of 88 percent, which district officials hope will climb further as the strengthened math pipeline takes effect.

Creston Valley University's board of trustees voted unanimously last month to freeze undergraduate tuition for the third consecutive academic year, holding the annual in-state rate at $12,840 and the out-of-state rate at $28,710. President Halvard Sorensson framed the decision as a deliberate affordability commitment made possible by a combination of strong endowment returns, a 6 percent increase in state appropriations, and administrative cost efficiencies realized through the university's ongoing operational review process. The endowment, currently valued at $1.14 billion, returned 9.3 percent in the most recent fiscal year, above the university's long-term actuarial assumption of 7 percent. Total undergraduate enrollment stands at 18,240, and financial aid expenditures for the current year reached $94.7 million, covering an average of 61 percent of demonstrated need for eligible students. Sorensson indicated that the tuition freeze, combined with a newly announced $8.2 million expansion of the university's institutional grant budget, should improve net-price affordability for families earning between $50,000 and $90,000 annually. The board also directed the provost's office to commission a comprehensive financial aid equity audit, with preliminary findings expected before the next trustee meeting in September.

The Dellmoor Institute of Technology has received accreditation renewal from the Northeastern Collegiate Accreditation Consortium for an additional ten-year period, the maximum term the consortium awards, following a rigorous four-day site visit conducted in February. The visiting team's report, released publicly last week, praised the institute's assessment practices, faculty development infrastructure, and student outcome data while recommending continued attention to graduate student mental health resources and advising caseloads in the College of Engineering. DIT's overall six-year graduation rate stands at 84 percent, and its first-destination employment rate for bachelor's degree recipients is 93 percent within six months of graduation. Interim Provost Celia Vandross said the renewal affirms the institution's commitment to quality and positions the institute favorably for several grant competitions that require current accreditation status. The Dellmoor Institute enrolls 9,600 students, of whom 3,100 are graduate students, and operates 14 doctoral programs across its four colleges. The Consortium noted in its report that DIT's student-to-faculty ratio of 14-to-1 and its consistent first-generation student retention rate of 79 percent were standout institutional strengths.

Falmont University's athletics department announced that its men's and women's rowing programs will be elevated from club to varsity status beginning with the 2026-2027 academic year, a transition that will bring the university's total varsity sport count to 24 and expand the number of athletic scholarships offered by 34. Athletic Director Rosalind Pemberton said the decision followed a two-year feasibility study showing sufficient regional competition infrastructure, strong student interest indicated by a current club roster of 87 athletes, and a committed fundraising base that has already pledged $3.8 million toward a new boathouse facility. The boathouse, to be constructed on the Aldmere River adjacent to campus, is designed to accommodate 14 boats and include an indoor ergometer training hall for winter months. Falmont's student body of 14,500 already supports strong engagement with water sports, and university officials project the varsity programs will attract 40 to 45 student-athletes per year once fully established. Conference placement talks are underway with the Lakeland Collegiate Sports Association. The women's program will be coached by nationally recognized sculling coach Beatrix Langford, who guided three athletes to national collegiate championships during her tenure at Greyhaven University.

The Pellwick Foundation announced the recipients of its 2026 Emerging Scholars Fellowship, awarding grants totaling $4.6 million to 18 doctoral students and early-career faculty at colleges and universities across the country. The fellowship, designed to support innovative interdisciplinary research at the intersection of technology and social equity, provides each recipient with $250,000 over two years to fund salary, research expenses, and conference dissemination. Among this year's cohort, four recipients are affiliated with Stonebridge University's School of Informatics, the highest concentration from any single institution in the fellowship's nine-year history. Pellwick Foundation President Maureen Aldgate said the 2026 class was selected from a pool of 847 applications, and that reviewers weighted both scholarly ambition and demonstrated commitment to community-engaged research methodologies. Recipients span fields including computational linguistics, urban data science, disability studies, and environmental justice. The foundation has awarded a cumulative total of $38.7 million through the Emerging Scholars program since its inception, supporting 164 scholars at 72 institutions. A symposium for the incoming cohort will be held in early September at the Pellwick Institute headquarters in Torrance Falls.

Moorgate Community College received a three-year, $7.9 million Title IV-equivalent workforce development grant from the state's Department of Postsecondary Education, allowing the college to launch or expand seven career and technical education pathways beginning in the fall semester. The grant will fund new program offerings in advanced manufacturing automation, healthcare informatics, sustainable construction practices, and supply chain logistics, each developed in direct partnership with regional employers who have committed to providing internship placements and hiring preference for graduates. College President Thaddeus Orrington said the funding also includes $1.4 million designated for equipment acquisition, covering the purchase of two industrial robotic systems, updated medical simulation laboratory technology, and a fully equipped building information modeling lab. Moorgate currently enrolls 6,800 credit-seeking students and serves an additional 2,300 through noncredit workforce programs annually. The college's completion rate has risen from 38 percent to 52 percent over the past five years following earlier investments in guided pathways redesign. State officials cited Moorgate as a model for regional workforce alignment, noting that 84 percent of the college's program completers who entered employment did so in fields directly related to their area of study.

Aldervane University dedicated the Miriam Stourbridge Center for Global Health last Friday in a ceremony attended by over 400 faculty, students, and community partners. The center, housed in a renovated 68,000-square-foot building that previously served as the university's administrative services hub, consolidates research and educational activities spanning epidemiology, global health policy, tropical medicine, and health systems strengthening. Funding for the $34.6 million renovation came primarily from a $22 million gift by the estate of Miriam Stourbridge, a physician-philanthropist and 1968 Aldervane alumna who devoted her career to infectious disease prevention in underserved communities. University President Edmund Calloway said the center will serve as an anchor for Aldervane's interdisciplinary doctoral program in global health, which admitted its inaugural cohort of 22 students this past January. The center will also house the university's new Stourbridge Clinical Simulation Suite, featuring 14 high-fidelity patient simulators used for training both medical students and community health workers. Research space within the facility accommodates 24 faculty investigators and over 60 graduate students, and administrators anticipate the center will generate more than $12 million in annual externally funded research activity within five years of full operation.

Glenport State University's Office of Institutional Research released its annual equity report this week, revealing that the university's four-year graduation rate for first-generation students reached 67 percent for the cohort entering in 2022, closing the gap with the overall institutional rate of 74 percent to the smallest margin ever recorded in the university's tracking history. The report attributed the improvement to the sustained investment in the Glenport BRIDGE Program, a comprehensive first-year intervention providing dedicated advising, peer mentoring, emergency financial aid, and supplemental instruction to approximately 1,100 incoming first-generation students per year. BRIDGE participants in the 2022 cohort were retained at a first-to-second year rate of 91 percent compared to 83 percent for first-generation students who did not enroll. Vice Provost for Student Success Imani Westbrook said the data reinforce the importance of high-touch, proactive support systems for students navigating higher education without family precedent. The university has invested $6.2 million annually in BRIDGE programming since expanding the model in 2021 and has received inquiries from 18 peer institutions interested in replicating elements of the approach. Glenport serves a total undergraduate population of 22,700 students, of whom 38 percent identify as first-generation.

The Lakeshore Charter Academy, a tuition-free public charter school serving grades six through twelve in the Overton Metropolitan School District, received a seven-year charter renewal from the state's Charter School Oversight Board following a comprehensive performance review. The board's evaluation found that the academy's students demonstrated statistically significant gains in reading proficiency and college-preparedness indicators relative to demographically comparable district schools, with 78 percent of twelfth graders meeting benchmarks for college readiness on the state's standardized assessments compared to a district average of 54 percent. Lakeshore currently enrolls 1,040 students, 72 percent of whom qualify for free or reduced-price meals, and has maintained a waitlist of over 600 students for the past four consecutive years. Executive Director Paloma Renwick credited the school's extended instructional day, a culture of high expectations, and strategic investment in teacher coaching and retention. The renewal allows the academy to expand its enrollment by up to 200 additional students over the charter term. The board did attach one condition to the renewal, requiring the academy to submit a detailed plan within 90 days outlining strategies to increase the proportion of students with disabilities fully included in general education classrooms.

Tremaine College announced a $14 million naming gift from the Albrecht-Fennel Family Foundation to establish the Albrecht-Fennel School of Environmental Stewardship, elevating what had been a freestanding academic department into a full school with the authority to award its own degree programs and hire faculty independently. The new school will launch two undergraduate majors — environmental policy and conservation science — alongside a professional master's program in sustainable systems management beginning in the 2027 academic year. Dean-designate Leonora Mayhew, currently associate provost at Thornfield University, will assume the school's leadership effective July 1. Mayhew has overseen curriculum development for three interdisciplinary environmental programs and brings a research background in watershed governance and voluntary carbon market regulation. Tremaine's president, Carlton Avery, said the gift and the school's formation reflect a long-term institutional commitment made in the college's 2023 strategic plan to make environmental education a signature strength. Total enrollment in the college's existing environmental studies courses already exceeds 900 students per semester, and early surveys of prospective students suggest the new programs will see strong initial demand, with a recruitment target of 120 new students in the inaugural undergraduate cohort.

Hartfield University's College of Education submitted a formal proposal to the State Board of Teacher Licensure to establish a new residency-based teacher preparation pathway that would allow candidates to earn initial licensure through a full academic year of paid classroom residency embedded within high-need partner schools. The proposed program, called the Hartfield Clinical Residency Initiative, would partner with the Brennan Unified School District and the Colewood City Schools, placing up to 80 resident teachers per cohort in classrooms alongside veteran mentor teachers who would receive $4,500 annual stipends for their coaching responsibilities. Residents would receive a living allowance of $18,000 for the residency year and would complete their remaining coursework in evening and weekend sessions. College of Education Dean Veronica Stahl presented the proposal to the licensure board at its quarterly meeting and noted that similar models at peer institutions have produced measurable improvements in first-year teacher retention, with residency graduates remaining in the classroom at rates 22 percent higher than traditionally prepared candidates after three years. The board is expected to render a decision within 60 days. If approved, the Hartfield Clinical Residency Initiative would admit its first cohort in the fall of 2027.

Portsmede University's annual research expenditure report revealed that the institution surpassed $300 million in total externally funded research activity for the first time in its history during fiscal year 2025, with $317.4 million in awards from federal agencies, private foundations, and industry partners. The milestone represents a 14 percent increase over the prior fiscal year and a 61 percent increase over the totals recorded a decade ago. Federal agency funding accounted for 68 percent of the total, with significant awards spanning biomedical research, materials science, computational social science, and environmental engineering. The university's Office of Research Development attributed the growth to a sustained strategy of investing in proposal development staff, fostering interdisciplinary collaborative teams, and building institutional infrastructure in areas aligned with federal funding priorities. Portsmede now ranks among the top 40 research universities nationally by total research expenditure, a standing that has improved from outside the top 70 just eight years ago. Vice President for Research Marcus Delgadillo said the university plans to add six new research-intensive faculty lines in identified high-growth areas during the upcoming hiring cycle and will commit $18 million from its research excellence fund to seed additional large-scale interdisciplinary proposals over the next three years.

The Summervale Unified School District's board of education voted 6-1 to adopt a universal pre-kindergarten initiative that will offer full-day, publicly funded early childhood education to all four-year-olds residing within district boundaries beginning with the 2026-2027 school year. The program, developed in collaboration with early childhood specialists from the Prentice Institute for Child Development, will initially serve approximately 1,800 children across 22 program sites, including both district-operated classrooms and contracted community-based partner providers. The district budgeted $11.4 million annually to fund the initiative, drawing on a combination of state early education block grant funds, federal early childhood allocations, and local general fund contributions. Superintendent Josephine Kincaid said the expansion was informed by a longitudinal study conducted by Summervale's research office showing that children who attended quality pre-K programs within the district were 31 percent more likely to read proficiently by third grade. The dissenting board member raised concerns about the sustainability of the funding model beyond the initial three-year grant period and requested that staff present a long-term fiscal analysis at the next regular board meeting. Enrollment for the inaugural program cohort will open in February.

Eastbrook University has promoted Dr. Yolanda Ferrara from associate professor to full professor in the Department of Computational Neuroscience, a promotion accompanied by the university's designation of Dr. Ferrara as a Dunmore Faculty Fellow, an honor reserved for scholars who have achieved exceptional distinction in research and have demonstrated commitments to mentoring. Dr. Ferrara's laboratory, which employs four postdoctoral researchers and eleven doctoral students, focuses on neural circuit models of decision-making under uncertainty, and her team's work has been supported by continuous external funding from the National Computational Sciences Fund for the past seven years totaling $8.6 million. She has authored 44 peer-reviewed publications, three of which appeared in the field's highest-impact venue, the Journal of Integrative Cognitive Systems. Her mentees have gone on to faculty positions at nine universities and to research roles at four major technology firms. The Dunmore Fellowship carries a $30,000 annual research supplement and invites the recipient to deliver the university's Dunmore Lecture, a public event drawing interdisciplinary faculty and students from across campus. Dean of Sciences Alistair Purvis praised Ferrara's promotion as recognition of scholarship that has positioned Eastbrook as a globally recognized center for computational approaches to brain and behavior research.

Rivermead College announced that its library system will undergo a $19.7 million transformation over the next three years, converting approximately 40,000 square feet of underutilized stack space in the Harlan Library into active learning studios, digital scholarship labs, and collaborative research suites. Funded through a combination of a $10 million gift from the Fenbrook Educational Trust and $9.7 million in capital campaign proceeds, the renovation will reduce physical holdings from 1.2 million volumes to 600,000, with the remaining items transferred to a high-density automated storage facility located one mile from campus and accessible through a same-day retrieval system. College Librarian Daphne Colquitt emphasized that the project responds to data showing that in-person borrowing of physical items has declined by 73 percent over the past decade while demand for collaborative study and digital production space has increased sharply. The redesigned Harlan Library will include a 300-seat event and lecture hall, a digitization center capable of processing archival materials for online access, and a technology lending program expanding the current inventory of loaned devices from 400 to 1,200. The project's first phase, covering the renovation of the library's east wing, is scheduled to begin in January with an expected completion date of August 2027.

The Brentfield Polytechnic Institute recognized fourteen undergraduate students with the Chancellor's Innovation Prize at its annual research showcase, awarding each honoree a $5,000 cash prize, a $2,000 professional development stipend, and a faculty-mentored pathway to apply the winning project toward patent or commercialization support through the university's Startup Incubation Hub. This year's top prize went to third-year student Dominic Yarborough for developing a low-cost soil moisture sensor network designed for smallholder farming applications, a system he prototyped and tested over two semesters in partnership with an agricultural cooperative 40 miles from campus. Additional honored projects addressed topics including portable water purification systems, accessible prosthetic device components, AI-assisted building energy management, and community noise mapping using distributed microphone arrays. The institute's provost, Harriet Vossler, noted that all 14 winning students were recipients of the Brentfield Applied Sciences Scholarship, suggesting a link between scholarship-funded research time and exceptional project outcomes. The Innovation Prize program, now in its eleventh year, has recognized 128 students whose projects collectively generated 22 patents, 9 startup ventures, and $3.4 million in follow-on entrepreneurial funding since the program's founding.

Caldwell University disclosed this week that its most recent comprehensive fundraising campaign concluded with a final total of $623 million, surpassing the campaign's publicly announced goal of $500 million and setting a new institutional record by a margin of $174 million over the previous campaign's results. The campaign, called Caldwell Forward: A Campaign for the Future, ran for seven years and drew gifts from 41,200 individual donors, a participation figure that university officials said exceeded projections by nearly 30 percent. Major campaign achievements included the establishment of 47 new endowed scholarships totaling $68 million in scholarship endowment, the creation of 12 new endowed professorships, $142 million committed to facilities construction and renovation, and $198 million designated for academic program innovation and research infrastructure. President Constance Halloway credited the outcome to strong volunteer leadership, a dedicated campaign staff, and a compelling vision for institutional investment that resonated with the university's 92,000 living alumni. The campaign also raised $31 million for the university's annual fund, which supports financial aid, faculty development, and student programs. Caldwell's endowment has grown from $820 million at the campaign's launch to $1.47 billion at present, a reflection of both gift inflows and strong investment returns.

The Granville School of Law at Westhaven University received notice of full accreditation reaffirmation from the Collegiate Legal Education Standards Commission, following a scheduled comprehensive review that the commission's team completed in March. The commission's report recognized the law school's bar passage rates, which averaged 86 percent for first-time takers across the past three years, well above the national median, and praised the school's robust clinical education offerings, which provided 312 students with hands-on legal representation experience in the most recent academic year. The school's 14 legal clinics served over 1,900 low-income clients during that period. Areas for continued improvement identified in the commission's report included enhancing diversity among adjunct faculty and strengthening career services resources for students pursuing public interest careers. Dean of the Law School Geraldine Mossworth said the reaffirmation provides confidence as the school moves into the second phase of its five-year strategic plan, which includes launching a new concentration in technology and intellectual property law and establishing two new clinics focused on housing rights and immigration. Current enrollment stands at 640 JD students and 88 graduate law students pursuing LLM degrees.

The Pelham County School System announced a new policy requiring all public middle and high school libraries to maintain a minimum curated collection of 2,000 titles per school, with at least 15 percent of holdings refreshed within the past five years. The policy, passed by the county board of education on a 9-3 vote, comes in response to an internal audit that found median school library collections had not received significant investment in seven years, with 34 percent of holdings published before 2005. District Superintendent Marta Kellum framed the initiative as part of a broader effort to improve reading engagement and information literacy outcomes, citing research showing strong correlations between robust school library programs and student reading achievement. The board allocated $2.6 million in one-time funds to bring all 41 school libraries into compliance with the new standard and committed to an annual library materials budget of $800,000 going forward, a 220 percent increase over current spending levels. The policy also establishes minimum certification requirements for school librarians, requiring all newly hired library staff to hold a master's degree in library science or a closely related field. Implementation of the staffing requirements will be phased in over three years to allow the district to manage the transition.

Northgate University's Department of Economics announced the hire of Dr. Penelope Ashcroft as an associate professor with tenure, a lateral hire representing a significant recruiting success for the department following a national search that attracted 94 applications. Dr. Ashcroft, who holds a doctorate from the Kellmore Institute for Economic Research and spent the past six years on the faculty of Brandtwell University, specializes in labor market dynamics, specifically the effects of automation and skill-biased technological change on wage structures in mid-sized metropolitan economies. Her research has been funded by the Langmore Policy Foundation and the Bureau of Economic Studies Grant Program, accumulating $2.3 million in external support over her career to date. She has published 31 peer-reviewed articles, including work appearing in the Review of Economic Dynamics and the Journal of Applied Labor Economics. Chair of the Economics Department, Professor Howard Saville, said Dr. Ashcroft's arrival strengthens the department's empirical labor economics cluster, which now comprises four faculty members whose work is nationally recognized in the field. Northgate's economics program currently enrolls 890 undergraduate majors and 142 graduate students and maintains one of the institution's highest post-graduation employment rates at 96 percent within one year of degree completion.

The Statewide Articulation Task Force on Higher Education Transfer released its final report recommending that all public colleges and universities in the state adopt a common lower-division general education framework comprising 36 credit hours guaranteed to transfer fully toward any baccalaureate degree at any public institution in the system. The report, the product of two years of deliberation among faculty representatives, academic officers, and student advocates from 34 institutions, found that transfer students who completed a structured associate degree before transferring graduated at four-year institutions at rates 11 percentage points higher than those who transferred without the structured degree. State higher education commissioner Doreen Halverson said the recommendations represent the most significant systemic reform of transfer policy in the state's history and pledged that her office would work with institutional governing boards to implement the framework within 24 months. The task force's report also recommended enhanced reverse transfer policies, allowing students who leave four-year institutions before completing their degree to receive retroactive associate degree credit for completed coursework. Implementation is expected to benefit approximately 28,000 transfer students annually who move between two-year and four-year institutions within the state system.

Moorwich University dedicated the Andress Hall of Innovation last Thursday, a 58,000-square-foot facility that serves as the physical home of the university's Andress Institute for Entrepreneurship and Technology Commercialization. The building was funded through a $26.5 million lead gift from entrepreneur and alumnus Frederick Andress, matched by $9.2 million from the university's capital reserves. The structure features 12 startup incubation suites, two makerspace fabrication labs, four corporate partnership research pods, and a 250-seat auditorium designed for pitch competitions and industry convenings. Since its soft launch in temporary facilities 18 months ago, the Andress Institute has supported 23 student and faculty startup ventures, three of which have secured external seed funding totaling $4.8 million. Provost Ramona Vickers said Andress Hall will allow the institute to more than double its programming, including adding a second cohort to its year-long Founder in Residence program, which provides selected ventures with office space, legal and accounting support, and access to a mentor network of 140 industry advisors. University officials anticipate the institute will support at least 60 active ventures within three years of full operation and project that Andress Hall will become a central node in the region's entrepreneurial ecosystem.

The Brennwood Independent School District announced a comprehensive revision to its teacher compensation model, replacing a decades-old single-salary schedule with a differentiated structure that accounts for teaching assignment, professional development attainment, and evaluated classroom effectiveness. Under the new model, which the board approved 5-2 and will phase in over three years, experienced teachers who earn an advanced instructional leadership designation and demonstrate high effectiveness ratings will be eligible for annual salaries reaching $92,000, compared to the previous maximum of $74,000. The district also created a $3.2 million Teacher Excellence Fund to support the new compensation tiers and associated professional development pathways. Superintendent Gareth Mallory said the goal is to make Brennwood one of the highest-paying districts for effective, experienced teachers in the state and to reduce turnover, which has run at 19 percent annually for the past four years. The district's teacher union negotiated several modifications to the initial proposal, including adding a defined career pathway for veteran teachers who prefer to remain in the classroom rather than take on leadership roles. Brennwood employs approximately 1,400 teachers across its 24 schools and serves 18,600 students. Union leadership ultimately endorsed the final agreement after modifications were incorporated.

Harwick University's School of Public Health received a five-year, $18.7 million program grant from the Delacroix Family Foundation to establish the Delacroix Center for Environmental Health Equity, a research and community engagement hub focused on the disproportionate burden of environmental hazards in low-income and minority communities. The center will support an interdisciplinary team of 14 faculty investigators, 18 doctoral fellows, and 30 undergraduate research assistants working across the schools of public health, law, social work, and urban planning. Founding director Dr. Antoinette Burnham, a nationally recognized researcher in cumulative environmental risk assessment, said the center will pursue a combination of epidemiological research, policy advocacy, and community-engaged science that translates findings directly into actionable interventions in partner communities. The Delacroix grant also funds a competitive small-grants program making 12 awards of $25,000 each per year to community organizations conducting their own environmental health monitoring. Harwick's president, Sylvester Okafor, described the award as foundational to a university-wide strategic priority of anchoring its public health research in questions of justice and equity. The center is expected to leverage the Delacroix funding to pursue additional federal and foundation grants totaling an estimated $12 million over the same five-year period.

The Tillmore Institute for Advanced Study in the Humanities announced the 2026-2027 class of residential fellows, a cohort of 16 scholars who will spend the academic year in residence at the institute's retreat campus in the Kellbrook foothills pursuing book-length projects, archival research, and interdisciplinary exchange. The cohort was selected from 612 applications and spans disciplines including medieval history, classical philosophy, postcolonial literature, performance theory, and architectural history. Each fellow receives a stipend of $72,000 for the year, housing in institute-provided faculty cottages, research expense support of $8,500, and access to the institute's 240,000-volume research library and digital humanities lab. Three of this year's 16 fellows are doctoral students in their final writing year, the remainder being mid-career and senior faculty on sabbatical leave from their home institutions. The institute's director, Professor Margaux Thibault, said the cohort reflects a deliberate effort to include scholars whose work engages non-Western textual and cultural traditions, with six of the 16 fellows focusing primarily on African, South Asian, or East Asian humanities subjects. The Tillmore Institute, founded in 1949, has supported over 2,100 residential fellowships since its establishment and counts among its alumni scholars who have produced 380 prize-winning books.

Sandhurst College announced that it has achieved carbon neutrality in its direct operations, becoming the first liberal arts college in its regional consortium to do so and the 34th institution of any type nationally to make the certification. The achievement required a decade-long investment totaling $31.4 million across energy efficiency retrofits, renewable energy procurement, and the installation of 1.8 megawatts of on-campus solar generation capacity. The college also contracted 4.1 megawatts of off-campus wind energy through a long-term power purchase agreement to offset electricity demand that cannot be met through on-campus generation. President Eloise Pemberton said carbon neutrality reflects an institutional conviction that colleges have an obligation to model the transitions they teach, and that the achievement has already become a recruiting asset, with surveys indicating that 41 percent of prospective students rated environmental sustainability practices as an important factor in their college choice. Sandhurst's next phase of climate commitment involves scope three emissions, covering supply chains, student and employee commuting, and air travel for academic purposes, with a goal of reaching institution-wide net-zero by 2038. The college has engaged the Greenfield Campus Sustainability Collaborative to develop a scope three measurement methodology and reduction plan.

Bridgemont State University's College of Nursing and Health Sciences received initial accreditation for its new Doctor of Nursing Practice program from the Collegiate Nursing Education Standards Council, clearing the way for the first cohort of 24 students to begin coursework in the fall semester. The DNP program, which will be offered in a hybrid format combining intensive weekend residencies with online coursework, is designed primarily for working registered nurses seeking advanced practice credentials in nurse anesthesia, nurse midwifery, or healthcare systems leadership. Program Director Dr. Sheila Denton said Bridgemont's program fills a significant regional workforce gap, as the nearest comparable program is currently operating at 140 percent capacity with a two-year waitlist. The program was developed over four years with substantial input from regional health systems, all three of which have agreed to provide supervised clinical placement sites for students. The university has committed $4.6 million in startup funding to hire four additional full-time clinical faculty, acquire simulation and clinical skills laboratory equipment, and develop the program's library of online instructional resources. The Nursing and Health Sciences College currently enrolls 1,240 students across its six existing programs and has a first-time NCLEX pass rate of 94 percent.

The Olmstead Preparatory Academy in the Colfield City School District celebrated the achievement of having 100 percent of its 212-member graduating class accepted to a two- or four-year college or university for the fourth consecutive year, with 89 percent of those acceptances at four-year institutions and 34 seniors earning acceptances at what the school designates as highly selective institutions. The class collectively earned $7.2 million in merit-based scholarship awards from their colleges and universities, up from $5.8 million for the prior year's class. Notable individual achievements included four students earning full four-year scholarship packages through competitive national programs administered by the Dellmore Collegiate Fund, each valued at approximately $280,000. Principal Darius McAllister said the results reflect a school culture built on the conviction that every student, regardless of background, can aspire to and achieve at the highest academic levels. The academy serves 840 students in grades nine through twelve, 68 percent of whom come from households qualifying for free or reduced-price meals. The school employs 62 full-time teachers with a median of 11 years of experience, and average class size is 21 students. The academy operates under a five-year performance compact with the district that ties continued operation to sustained achievement outcomes.

Whitmore University announced the establishment of the Calloway Center for Democratic Institutions, a nonpartisan policy research center funded by a $12 million founding gift from the estate of former circuit court judge Reginald Calloway, a 1962 Whitmore alumnus who served for 28 years on the federal bench. The center will commission and publish research on the health and functioning of democratic governance structures at local, state, and national levels, with a particular focus on judicial independence, electoral systems integrity, and the institutional conditions for effective legislative deliberation. Founding Director Professor Lena Hartwick, recruited from her position as director of constitutional studies at the Fordmere Policy Institute, said the center will maintain strict methodological and editorial independence, publishing findings regardless of their political implications. The Calloway Center will host an annual symposium bringing together legal scholars, political scientists, and civic practitioners, the first of which is planned for March of next year. A graduate certificate in democratic governance is being developed in conjunction with Whitmore's law school and political science department, with enrollment expected to begin in the 2027-2028 academic year. The center will also administer four annual dissertation fellowships of $20,000 each for doctoral students whose research bears on democratic institutional questions.

The Pelrose County community college district approved a $145 million bond measure implementation plan that will fund the construction of a new allied health sciences building, a student services hub, and a performing arts center across its three campuses over the next seven years. Voters approved the bond measure in November by a 61 percent majority, and the district's board of trustees ratified the final spending plan following an 18-month community engagement and planning process that included more than 40 public input sessions attended collectively by over 3,200 community members. The allied health sciences building at the district's main Pelrose campus will be the largest of the three major projects at $58 million, and will house programs in nursing, diagnostic imaging, dental hygiene, physical therapy assistant, and emergency medical services. District Chancellor Roderick Nwamba said the bond represents the most significant infrastructure investment in the district's 67-year history and reflects community confidence in the colleges' role as workforce and civic assets. Current combined enrollment across the three campuses stands at 19,800 credit students. The district has contracted Stanton Construction Group to manage all three major projects, with groundbreaking on the health sciences building scheduled for January.

Cloverdale University's Department of History received the Vanthorpe Award for Departmental Excellence in Undergraduate Teaching from the Council for Historical Education, an honor awarded annually to one history department nationally. The award, which carries a $50,000 prize to be used for student programming and pedagogical development, recognized the department's innovative core curriculum requirement, which asks all history majors to complete a rigorous archival research project during their second year rather than waiting until the senior thesis. The department chair, Professor Edwina Colchester, said the early archival experience has measurably improved thesis quality, with external evaluators rating the department's senior theses an average of 0.7 points higher on a five-point scale since the curriculum change was implemented six years ago. History enrollments at Cloverdale have grown from 210 to 310 declared majors over the same period despite a national trend of declining humanities enrollments, a fact the Council cited explicitly in its award rationale. The department employs 18 full-time faculty, including five who have won the university's teaching excellence award in the past decade. The Vanthorpe Award funds will support expanded summer archival fellowships that will allow 15 students per year to conduct original research at off-campus archives with travel and housing support.

Dunmore Graduate University's professional master's program in applied statistics announced that its placement outcomes for the most recent graduating class set a new record, with 100 percent of the 88 graduates securing employment or additional graduate study within four months of degree completion. The median starting salary for graduates entering industry positions was $112,000, an increase of 9 percent over the prior year's cohort. Employers represented in the placement data included technology firms, healthcare analytics companies, financial services organizations, and government statistical agencies. Program Director Dr. Francesca Limon attributed the outcomes to a deliberate curriculum design that integrates industry partnership throughout the degree, including a required six-month practicum with a sponsor organization, a capstone project conducted in collaboration with one of the program's 24 employer partners, and a dedicated career development sequence taught by practitioners. The program is highly selective, admitting 90 students per year from a pool that in the most recent cycle exceeded 1,100 applications. Dunmore's provost, Martin Kelloway, said the program has become a national model for professional science master's education and is frequently cited by peer institutions exploring similar offerings. Three partner organizations have recently expanded their annual practicum commitments from one student to three.

The Riverside Academy of Arts and Sciences, a specialized public high school serving 620 students in the Falcrest City School District, received a $1.8 million implementation grant from the Stanhope Foundation for Educational Innovation to develop a vertically integrated arts-and-STEM curriculum spanning all four grade levels. The grant funds the creation of 14 new interdisciplinary course modules, the hiring of three additional faculty members with joint expertise in creative and technical domains, and the construction of a 4,200-square-foot design and fabrication studio. Principal Tomoko Haverfield said the project builds on the academy's existing strength in project-based learning, with the new curriculum creating explicit connective tissue between disciplines that students previously experienced in parallel but separate tracks. Students admitted to the academy are selected through a competitive portfolio and audition process, and 78 percent come from households below the median family income for the district. The Stanhope grant also funds a three-year research partnership with Falcrest Community College's School of Education to evaluate student creative problem-solving outcomes and to document the curriculum design process for potential replication. Interim findings from the research will be shared at regional arts education conferences beginning in the spring of next year.

Beaufort University announced that Provost Claudette Moreau will step down at the end of the academic year after serving in the role for nine years, one of the longest tenures in the position in the university's history. During her tenure, Moreau oversaw the launch of 31 new academic programs, the completion of $340 million in campus construction projects, and a 22 percent increase in undergraduate enrollment, which now stands at 24,100 students. She also led the development of the university's current strategic plan, which has guided $78 million in academic investments over the past four years. President Anton Schwarzfeld praised Moreau's tenure as defined by principled decision-making, deep faculty trust, and an unwavering focus on student outcomes that improved six-year graduation rates from 71 percent to 83 percent under her leadership. A national search for her successor will begin immediately, led by a 14-member advisory committee comprising faculty, students, staff, and trustees. Moreau said she plans to take a sabbatical year before returning to faculty in Beaufort's Department of Organizational Management, where she holds a tenured professorship. She will be recognized at a university celebration in late May, at which the board of trustees is expected to announce a significant gift in her honor supporting a faculty development fund.

The faculty senate at Ironwood University voted 63-11 to endorse a new framework for evaluating and rewarding faculty engagement in community-engaged scholarship, creating a formal mechanism for community-based research and creative work to be submitted and assessed as part of tenure and promotion dossiers alongside traditional peer-reviewed publications. Developed over three years by a joint task force of faculty scholars and community partners, the framework provides evaluative criteria adapted from standards established by the Scholarship of Engagement Institute and includes protocols for how department promotion committees should assess the rigor, reach, and significance of community partnership work. Provost Delphine Armand said the framework represents a fundamental institutional statement that Ironwood values multiple forms of scholarly excellence and that faculty who invest deeply in community-engaged work deserve a fair and informed evaluation process. The framework will take effect for all promotion cases initiated in the 2026-2027 academic year. Ironwood has 780 full-time faculty across its seven colleges, of whom an estimated 120 regularly conduct community-engaged research. The senate's endorsement requires formal ratification by the board of trustees, which is expected at the board's June meeting.

Pemberton University received a $42 million gift from the Greystone Partners Charitable Fund, the largest single donation in the university's 134-year history, designated to endow 18 new graduate fellowships in science, technology, and mathematics fields. Each fellowship, to be named in honor of a different Greystone Partners employee-donor, will provide doctoral students with a five-year package including a $35,000 annual stipend, full tuition coverage, health insurance, and $3,500 per year for conference travel and research expenses. The 18 fellowships will be distributed across the College of Natural Sciences, the School of Engineering, and the Computational Sciences Division, with preference given to applicants from underrepresented groups as defined by the National Science Foundation's diversity categories. Vice President for Research Constance Alleyne said the fellowships will allow Pemberton to recruit at the very top of the national doctoral applicant pool in fields where competition for the most talented students has intensified significantly. The Greystone gift is the product of a four-year cultivation relationship and reflects the fund's conviction that investing in graduate education is among the highest-leverage philanthropic strategies for advancing scientific and technological progress. The first Greystone Fellows will be announced in February and will begin their programs the following fall.

The Marshbrook School District released the results of its biennial teaching quality survey, which drew responses from 94 percent of the district's 1,600 classroom teachers and yielded the highest overall satisfaction and professional engagement scores in the survey's 14-year history. Seventy-eight percent of respondents reported feeling well-supported by building-level instructional coaches, up from 62 percent in the prior survey cycle, a shift district officials attributed to a program that reduced average coaching caseloads from 42 to 28 teachers per coach over the past three years. Teacher retention improved from 84 percent to 91 percent in the same period, meaning that approximately 130 fewer teachers left the district annually than had done so previously. Superintendent Gwendolyn Asante said the data validate the district's sustained investment in professional support, which totaled $7.4 million in the current fiscal year. The survey also identified persistent concerns about planning time and class size in the district's three large comprehensive high schools, areas that the district's board of education will address in the upcoming budget deliberation cycle. Marshbrook serves 22,100 students across 34 schools and has a graduation rate of 90 percent.

Vantermere College of Art and Design appointed Dr. Helena Foss as its new president, effective August 1, filling a position that has been held in an interim capacity for the past 11 months. Dr. Foss arrives from Millhaven University, where she served as dean of the College of Fine Arts for seven years, overseeing a 40 percent increase in enrollment and the successful completion of a $68 million performing arts center. She holds a doctorate in art history from the Wexler Institute and a terminal degree in printmaking from the National Academy of Visual Arts, a background the search committee said makes her uniquely suited to lead an institution where artistic practice and academic rigor must be held in productive tension. Vantermere enrolls 2,400 students in programs spanning fine arts, graphic design, animation, industrial design, and arts administration, and has an endowment of $54 million. Board Chair Valentina Rossi said the trustees were drawn to Foss's blend of creative credentials, administrative accomplishment, and vision for expanding access to arts education, noting that Foss made increasing enrollment of students from low-income backgrounds a centerpiece of her leadership at Millhaven. Her first stated priorities at Vantermere include strengthening industry partnerships, reviewing the financial aid model, and completing an accreditation self-study due in 2027.

Lakewood Community School District announced the launch of its Pathways to Industry Credentials initiative, a $6.3 million, four-year effort to expand dual enrollment and industry-recognized credential programs available to high school students. Under the initiative, students at all four of the district's comprehensive high schools will have access to at least eight stackable credential pathways in sectors identified through employer surveys as high-need, including healthcare support, information technology, advanced manufacturing, education and child development, and culinary and hospitality management. The district has partnered with Lakewood Valley Community College to deliver the dual enrollment academic coursework and with seven regional employers to provide structured work-based learning placements. Superintendent Blaire Ogundele said the goal is to ensure that every graduating senior who participates in a pathway completes at least one industry-recognized credential, dramatically expanding the post-secondary options available to students who may not immediately pursue a four-year degree. The initiative is funded through a combination of state workforce development grants, federal Perkins funds, and a $1.8 million contribution from the Lakewood Regional Business Partnership. Early enrollment projections suggest that approximately 1,100 students per year will participate once the initiative reaches full implementation in year three.

Carleton Heights University's office of undergraduate admissions reported that applications for the entering class of 2027 increased by 19 percent over the prior year, reaching 38,400 applications for approximately 2,800 seats in the incoming class. The increase, which follows a 12 percent gain in the prior cycle, has brought the university's overall acceptance rate below 9 percent for the first time in its history. Dean of Admissions Phillip Terrano said several factors contributed to the volume surge, including the university's decision to adopt test-optional admissions permanently following its pandemic-era pilot, expanded digital recruitment efforts, and heightened national visibility resulting from multiple faculty members receiving prominent research awards in the past year. The incoming class profile suggests a significant increase in geographic diversity, with applications from students in 14 states where Carleton Heights has historically had minimal presence rising by more than 35 percent. The admitted class will also reflect the highest proportion of Pell Grant-eligible students in at least two decades, projected at 24 percent, in part because of a new financial aid initiative that meets 100 percent of demonstrated need for admitted students from families earning below $75,000 annually. Carleton Heights currently enrolls 10,200 undergraduates and 3,400 graduate and professional students.

The Drandell Prize Committee announced that Professor Oswald Breckenridge of Summerfield College has been awarded the 2026 Drandell Prize in Moral Philosophy, the most prestigious recognition in the discipline within the domestic scholarly community, carrying a $100,000 honorarium and a residency at the Drandell Foundation campus in the highland town of Westmoraven. Breckenridge was recognized for his book The Texture of Obligation, a 420-page examination of the relationship between second-personal address, moral accountability, and the authority of practical reasons, which was published two years ago by Kellbridge University Press and has been widely reviewed as a generational contribution to the field. The prize committee described the work as philosophically rigorous, stylistically accessible, and genuinely consequential for debates spanning moral psychology, political philosophy, and normative ethics. Breckenridge has been on the faculty at Summerfield for 19 years and holds the Denvil Distinguished Chair in Philosophy, an endowed position established in 2014. He has supervised 22 doctoral students to completion, 14 of whom hold faculty positions at colleges and universities. Summerfield's provost, Catherine Laszlo, said the award reflects the intellectual vitality of what she called one of the finest philosophy departments at any liberal arts college in the country.

The Westport Unified School District's board of education voted unanimously to contract with the Corman Group, an independent educational auditing firm, to conduct a comprehensive review of the district's special education services following a state monitoring report that identified procedural compliance deficiencies in Individualized Education Program development and implementation. The audit, projected to cost $380,000 and take approximately eight months, will assess caseload distribution among special education staff, timeliness and quality of IEP documentation, the availability and appropriateness of least restrictive environment placements, and the adequacy of related services. Special education directors from neighboring districts will not be involved in the review given conflict-of-interest concerns. Superintendent Patricia Avon told the board that the district is committed to full transparency and will share the Corman Group's findings with the public and with the state education agency in unredacted form. The district currently serves 1,840 students with disabilities across its 19 schools, approximately 13 percent of total enrollment. State monitors found that 22 percent of sampled IEPs had procedural deficiencies, a rate the monitors characterized as elevated, though they noted no findings of substantive harm to students. The district has until the next state monitoring cycle in 18 months to demonstrate corrective action.

Meridian Technical University announced the formation of the Meridian-Solverra Industry Alliance, a formal research and talent development partnership with Solverra Energy Corporation, a regional firm specializing in grid-scale battery storage and distributed energy resource management. The five-year agreement, valued at $11.4 million in cash, equipment, and in-kind contributions, establishes two dedicated research laboratories at Meridian's College of Electrical and Systems Engineering, funds five annual corporate-sponsored graduate research fellowships, and creates a structured internship pathway placing at least 20 Meridian undergraduates per year at Solverra facilities. Solverra CEO Diana Kroft said the partnership addresses an acute engineering talent shortage the company faces as it scales its operations to meet regional clean energy infrastructure demand. University President Marcus Osei noted that the alliance also provides Meridian with access to proprietary operational data sets that will accelerate research in grid optimization algorithms, energy storage degradation modeling, and demand response system design. An intellectual property agreement accompanying the alliance specifies that Meridian retains publication rights to all research conducted in the alliance laboratories while Solverra receives first right of negotiation on commercialization opportunities. The first two graduate fellows will be selected and begin their supported research in September.

Ashton College announced the selection of 22 students for its inaugural Lindqvist Leadership Scholars cohort, a competitive fellowship supporting students committed to public service careers in government, nonprofit management, or community organizing. The Lindqvist Fellowship, established through a $6.8 million endowment gift from retired diplomat and alumnus Hector Lindqvist, provides each scholar with a $15,000 annual award, a structured leadership curriculum, a summer placement with a public or civic organization, and mentorship from a network of 85 Lindqvist alumni advisors drawn from across the public sector. Selection for the inaugural cohort was competitive, drawing 290 applications for 22 spots, with the selection committee prioritizing demonstrated commitment to community engagement and potential for leadership in public life. The scholars represent 12 declared majors and come from 14 different states, with 14 of the 22 identifying as first-generation college students. Director of Leadership Programs Emelia Sandstrom said the fellowship will create a sustained campus culture of public service aspiration and help Ashton fulfill its founding mission of educating engaged citizens. Scholars will participate in a two-credit leadership seminar each semester and will meet as a cohort for a four-day orientation retreat in late August before the academic year begins.

The Regional Accreditation Council for Graduate Management Education granted full accreditation to the Cortland School of Business at Westfield University, elevating the school into an elite tier that fewer than 7 percent of business schools worldwide have achieved. The accreditation process, which took four years and involved extensive documentation of learning outcomes, faculty qualifications, curricular rigor, and strategic planning coherence, culminated in a three-day site visit last October by an eight-member peer review team. In its final report, the review team highlighted the school's innovative approach to integrating ethics and sustainability throughout the core MBA curriculum, its strong alumni engagement network of over 14,000 graduates, and its record of continuous improvement in student outcome metrics. Cortland School of Business Dean Marguerite Lacroix said the accreditation opens significant new opportunities for international student recruitment, corporate partnerships, and research collaborations with peer institutions who require accredited counterparts for formal agreements. Current enrollment in the school's graduate programs stands at 640 students in MBA, specialized master's, and executive education formats, and the school projects enrollment will grow by 15 percent within three years as the accreditation's market signal takes effect. Westfield's president called the milestone the most significant institutional achievement of the decade.

Clearfield University's Department of Physical Education and Kinesiology published the results of its three-year evaluation of the campus-wide Wellspring Physical Activity Initiative, a program launched in 2023 that incentivized student participation in structured physical activity through a tiered reward system linked to campus recreation facilities access and small scholarship supplements. The evaluation, conducted by an independent team within the university's school of public health, found that students who participated consistently in Wellspring programming over all three years reported significantly higher self-rated physical health scores, missed an average of 1.8 fewer academic days per semester due to illness, and maintained a semester GPA an average of 0.22 points higher than a matched comparison group. Participation in the initiative grew from 3,100 students in its first year to 7,400 in the most recent year, representing approximately 41 percent of the full-time student body. The university's vice president for student affairs, Nathaniel Cordova, announced that the initiative will be made permanent and will receive increased funding of $1.9 million annually in the upcoming budget cycle. A companion initiative targeting graduate student physical activity is being piloted with 800 participants this semester.

The Aldermoor Commission on the Future of Higher Education, a 22-member panel convened by the state legislature and comprising university presidents, faculty representatives, student advocates, employers, and policy experts, released its final report recommending a comprehensive overhaul of the state's higher education funding formula. The report, the product of 14 months of hearings, data analysis, and deliberation, argues that the current formula, which distributes state appropriations based primarily on enrollment headcount, creates incentives misaligned with student success and institutional mission diversity. The commission recommends transitioning to a outcomes-informed funding model under which 40 percent of state appropriations would be tied to metrics including credential completion, transfer rates, student equity outcomes, and graduate employment in high-need fields. The remaining 60 percent would continue as mission-based base funding, protecting institutional stability. Commission Chair Yvonne Haralson, president of Northfield Regional College, said the model balances accountability with the autonomy institutions need to serve diverse student populations. The report projects that implementation of the recommended formula would direct approximately $140 million in annual state appropriations differently than the current formula, with community colleges and regional comprehensive universities generally benefiting and some research universities experiencing modest reductions. The legislature's Higher Education Committee has scheduled a series of public hearings to review the recommendations before any legislation is drafted.
